events.text: The patient is a 63-year-old female named Margaret Okonkwo. She is of Nigerian-American descent, a non-smoker, with a history of well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus (on metformin), and hypertension (on amlodipine). She is a retired nurse. BMI 29.4. ECOG performance status 1 at presentation.

At age 63 years, the patient had a diagnosis of stage IV (AJCC 8th edition) clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) of the right kidney (ICD-O site code C64.0), histology code 8310/3 (clear cell adenocarcinoma). Primary tumor: T3b (tumor extends into the right renal vein with macroscopic venous thrombus). Regional lymph nodes: N1 (single retroperitoneal lymph node metastasis, 2.4 cm). Distant metastases: M1 (bilateral pulmonary nodules, largest 1.8 cm right lower lobe). Sarcomatoid differentiation present in approximately 20% of the tumor. Fuhrman nuclear grade: Grade 4. Tumor size: 14.2 cm at greatest dimension. Summary stage: Distant (Stage IV). IMDC risk score: Intermediate risk (2 factors: hemoglobin below lower limit of normal, time from diagnosis to systemic therapy less than 1 year). Confirmation: Positive histology via surgical pathology from cytoreductive nephrectomy. Relevant biomarkers: PD-L1 expression by combined positive score (CPS) 12, tumor mutational burden (TMB) low (3.1 mut/Mb). No evidence of hereditary RCC syndrome (no germline VHL, FLCN, MET, SDHB/C/D mutations detected).

At age 63 years (initial presentation), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer. Findings: Large heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 14.2 x 11.8 x 10.3 cm arising from the upper and mid pole of the right kidney with areas of central necrosis and calcification. Macroscopic tumor thrombus extending into the right renal vein (grade 2 Mayo thrombus). Right retroperitoneal lymph node measuring 2.4 x 1.9 cm at the level of the renal hilum. No adrenal involvement. Liver unremarkable. Bilateral pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 1.8 cm, right lower lobe 0.9 cm, left lower lobe 0.7 cm, and two additional subcentimeter nodules bilaterally. No pleural effusion. No skeletal metastases identified. No brain metastases on dedicated MRI brain (performed separately). Impression: Right renal mass consistent with primary renal cell carcinoma, stage IV disease with regional lymphadenopathy and bilateral pulmonary metastases.

At age 63 years (initial presentation), the patient had an MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast, which showed no cancer. Findings: No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Normal brain parenchyma. No midline shift or mass effect. Impression: No intracranial metastases.

At age 63 years, the patient had a CT-guided core needle biopsy of the primary right renal mass prior to surgical planning. Pathology type: ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY. Specimen: Right renal mass core biopsy (3 cores). Diagnosis: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma, Fuhrman grade 4, with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation. Microscopic description: The tumor is composed predominantly of nests and sheets of clear cells with abundant clear cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli (grade 4 features). In approximately 20% of the sampled tissue, there is transition to sarcomatoid morphology with spindled cells, high mitotic rate, and geographic necrosis. No rhabdoid differentiation identified. Immunohistochemistry: Tumor cells positive for CA9 (diffuse), PAX8 (positive), RCC antigen (positive), vimentin (positive in sarcomatoid component), CK8/18 (focal, predominantly in clear cell component). CD10 positive. TFE3 negative. CAIX strongly and diffusely positive. PD-L1 (22C3 antibody): CPS 12. Ki-67 proliferative index: approximately 35% in sarcomatoid component, 12% in clear cell component.

At age 63 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for clear cell renal cell carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the right renal mass core biopsy (primary tumor). A comprehensive tumor genomic profiling panel (Foundation One CDx equivalent, 324-gene panel) was performed. Actionable/clinically relevant alterations: VHL p.R200* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, VAF 48%, homozygous loss with concurrent LOH chromosome 3p), PBRM1 p.Q1233* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, VAF 42%), SETD2 frameshift deletion (exon 12, p.K1340fs, VAF 31%), TP53 p.R175H (hotspot missense, pathogenic, VAF 28%), MET amplification (copy number 8.2, high-level amplification). Additional alterations: KDM5C p.D870G (missense, variant of uncertain significance, X-linked, VAF 89%), ARID1A p.R1989W (missense, VUS, VAF 22%), MTOR p.L1460P (missense, activating, VAF 19% - subclonal), BAP1 diploid (wild-type). Copy number alterations: Loss of chromosome 3p (encompassing VHL, PBRM1, SETD2, BAP1 locus), gain of chromosome 5q, loss of chromosome 14q. Fusions: No clinically significant gene fusions detected. Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). Tumor mutational burden: 3.1 mutations per megabase (low). PD-L1 (22C3): CPS 12. Germline findings: No reportable germline alterations in VHL, FLCN, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD, MET, or PTEN. Note: MET amplification is of particular interest in the context of sarcomatoid differentiation and may have therapeutic relevance.

At age 63, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) assessment at a tertiary academic medical center. Present: urologic oncology, medical oncology, thoracic surgery, interventional radiology, and radiation oncology. Discussion: Patient presents with synchronous metastatic ccRCC with large primary tumor (T3b, renal vein involvement), single-station retroperitoneal nodal disease, and limited bilateral pulmonary metastatic burden. Performance status ECOG 1. The sarcomatoid component (20%) and Fuhrman grade 4 histology are concerning for aggressive biology. Noted: MET amplification on NGS - discussed potential future relevance for cabozantinib sensitivity. The MDT consensus recommendation was for upfront cytoreductive nephrectomy given: (1) good performance status, (2) symptomatic primary tumor (flank pain, hematuria), (3) limited M1 disease burden, and (4) IMDC intermediate risk. Following surgical recovery, initiation of systemic therapy with ipilimumab plus nivolumab (CheckMate 214 regimen) was recommended given sarcomatoid histology (pre-specified subgroup with improved benefit from IO combination). Cancer present. No response assessment yet - pre-treatment.

At age 63 years, the patient underwent a right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (cytoreductive intent). Approach: Open (hand-assisted) via a right flank incision. Procedure details: Right kidney with tumor (14.2 cm), right renal vein with tumor thrombus (completely resected), right adrenal gland (adherent, included en bloc), and right retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (template: right paracaval, interaortocaval, right common iliac). Estimated blood loss: 850 mL. Intraoperative transfusion: 2 units packed red blood cells. Operative time: 4 hours 10 minutes. No intraoperative complications. Margins: Negative (R0 resection of primary). Tumor thrombus: Completely resected with negative renal vein margin.

At age 63 years, the patient had a surgical pathology report from the right radical nephrectomy specimen. Pathology type: ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY. Specimen: Right kidney, right adrenal gland, and retroperitoneal lymph nodes. Final diagnosis: (A) Right kidney: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma, Fuhrman nuclear grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation comprising 20% of the tumor volume. Tumor size: 14.2 x 11.6 x 10.1 cm. Pathologic T stage: pT3b (macroscopic venous extension into renal vein). Lymphovascular invasion: Present. Perirenal fat invasion: Present. Adrenal gland: No tumor involvement. Surgical margins: All margins negative (R0). (B) Retroperitoneal lymph nodes: 1 of 12 lymph nodes positive for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (the 2.4 cm right hilar node); 11/12 nodes negative. Largest positive node: 2.4 cm, with extranodal extension. Pathologic N stage: pN1. (C) Right adrenal gland: No metastatic carcinoma. Fibrous adhesion to the upper pole tumor noted. Additional IHC confirmatory stains: CA9 positive, PAX8 positive, TFE3 negative, consistent with clear cell RCC. Overall pathologic stage: pT3b pN1 M1, Stage IV.

At age 63 years (postoperative day 4), the patient developed a postoperative complication: ileus (Grade 2 per CTCAE v5.0). Managed conservatively with bowel rest, nasogastric tube decompression, and IV fluid support. Resolved by postoperative day 8. Additional postoperative finding: mild anemia (hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, Grade 1) managed with oral iron supplementation. Hospital course otherwise unremarkable. Discharged on postoperative day 10.

At age 63 years (6 weeks post-nephrectomy), the patient had a post-surgical medical oncology assessment. Patient has recovered well from surgery. ECOG performance status improved to 1. Laboratory results: Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (improving), creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR 46 mL/min/1.73m2, expected given solitary kidney status), LDH 198 U/L (mildly elevated, upper limit of normal 190), corrected calcium 9.4 mg/dL (normal), neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio 3.2. IMDC risk score reassessed: Intermediate risk (2 factors - anemia, time from diagnosis to systemic therapy <12 months). Plan: Initiate first-line systemic therapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x 4 cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance. Baseline imaging obtained. Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed. Cancer present (residual pulmonary metastases). No response to treatment yet - initiating therapy.

At age 63 years (baseline pre-systemic therapy, 6 weeks post-nephrectomy), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast serving as restaging/baseline imaging. Cancer present. Findings: Status post right nephrectomy. No local recurrence at the nephrectomy bed. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (previously positive node resected). Pulmonary metastases: right lower lobe dominant nodule 1.8 cm (target lesion 1), right lower lobe second nodule 0.9 cm (target lesion 2), left lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (target lesion 3). Two additional subcentimeter nodules (non-target). No new sites of disease. No pleural effusion. No hepatic lesions. Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.4 cm. Impression: Metastatic disease limited to bilateral pulmonary nodules. Suitable for RECIST 1.1 response assessment.

At age 63 years, the patient received first-line immunotherapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (CheckMate 214 regimen), initiated approximately 6 weeks following cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy. Intent: palliative/disease control. Rationale: IMDC intermediate risk, sarcomatoid differentiation (pre-specified subgroup with enhanced benefit from dual checkpoint inhibition in CheckMate 214), PD-L1 CPS 12. Premedications: Ondansetron, acetaminophen. Baseline thyroid function, glucose, LFTs, and CBC obtained and within acceptable limits.

At age 63 years (after cycle 2 of ipilimumab/nivolumab, approximately week 6 of systemic therapy), the patient developed immune-related colitis (irAE). Symptoms: watery diarrhea, 5-7 loose stools per day, mild lower abdominal cramping, no blood in stool. CTCAE Grade 2 diarrhea/colitis. Managed with: loperamide (initially, without response), then oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with slow taper over 8 weeks. Ipilimumab held (permanently discontinued after Grade 2 immune-related colitis per protocol); nivolumab continued after steroid taper completed (per institutional protocol and patient preference after shared decision-making). Colonoscopy performed: diffuse pan-colitis without ulceration consistent with immune-related colitis. Infectious workup negative (C. difficile, CMV colitis excluded). Symptoms resolved to Grade 1 by week 3 of steroids. Complete resolution to Grade 0 by week 7 of prednisone taper.

At age 63 years (approximately 3 months after initiating systemic therapy, after 4 cycles of combination then initiating nivolumab monotherapy maintenance), the patient had a medical oncology assessment with first restaging imaging. Cancer present. Mixed response observed. ECOG performance status 1. Patient reports fatigue (Grade 1), improved energy over the past 2 weeks since steroid taper completed. No further diarrhea. Thyroid function tests: TSH 7.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated), free T4 low-normal - consistent with subclinical immune-related hypothyroidism; levothyroxine 50 mcg daily initiated. Restaging CT: mixed response per RECIST 1.1 - target lesion 1 (RLL) slightly increased to 2.1 cm (pseudoprogression vs. true progression being considered given immunotherapy context), target lesions 2 and 3 decreased (0.7 cm and 0.5 cm respectively). Sum of diameters 3.3 cm (stable/minimal change). Decision: Continue nivolumab, repeat imaging in 6 weeks to assess for pseudoprogression vs. true progression. Cancer present with mixed response.

At age 63 years (3-month restaging scan on nivolumab monotherapy after completing 4 cycles ipilimumab/nivolumab induction), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Cancer present. Mixed response. Target lesion 1 (RLL dominant): 2.1 cm (from 1.8 cm baseline, +17% increase). Target lesion 2 (RLL): 0.7 cm (from 0.9 cm, -22% decrease). Target lesion 3 (LLL): 0.5 cm (from 0.7 cm, -29% decrease). Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.3 cm (from 3.4 cm baseline, -3% change, RECIST stable disease). One of the previously non-target subcentimeter nodules has increased to 0.5 cm (remains non-target). No new sites of disease. No pleural effusion. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No adrenal or hepatic lesions. Impression: Mixed response with heterogeneous behavior of pulmonary metastases. Overall RECIST assessment: Stable disease. Clinical team to consider pseudoprogression of target lesion 1 in immunotherapy context.

At age 63 years (6-month restaging scan, confirmatory scan after mixed response), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Cancer present. Findings: Target lesion 1 (RLL dominant): 2.6 cm (from 1.8 cm baseline, +44% increase from baseline; from 2.1 cm prior scan, +24% increase - unambiguous progression of this lesion). Target lesion 2 (RLL): 0.6 cm (stable). Target lesion 3 (LLL): 0.5 cm (stable). Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.7 cm (from 3.4 cm baseline, +9% change). New finding: Two new pulmonary nodules right middle lobe measuring 0.6 cm and 0.4 cm. No new extrapulmonary disease. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy: New right paracaval lymph node measuring 1.1 cm (new since baseline). Impression: Mixed response with continued progression in target lesion 1 and new sites of disease (new pulmonary nodules, new retroperitoneal lymph node). Progressive disease per RECIST 1.1 criteria (new lesions).

At age 63 years (6 months into systemic therapy, progressive disease on nivolumab monotherapy), the patient had a medical oncology assessment for disease progression. Cancer present. Progressing. ECOG performance status 1 (unchanged). The patient has progressive disease per RECIST 1.1 on confirmatory scan at 6 months (new pulmonary nodules and new retroperitoneal lymph node). Although the predominant response pattern was mixed (some lesions regressing), the emergence of new sites of disease and progression of the dominant pulmonary metastasis confirm true progression. Plan: Discontinue nivolumab. Transition to second-line therapy with cabozantinib 60 mg orally once daily (per CABOSUN rationale and MET amplification on NGS making cabozantinib particularly attractive - dual VEGFR/MET inhibitor). Discussed clinical trial options; no immediately eligible trials at this institution. Discussed METEOR trial data for cabozantinib as second-line post-IO therapy. Baseline labs prior to cabozantinib initiation: CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH, UA. Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL. Liver function normal. Initiate cabozantinib with close monitoring for hand-foot skin reaction, diarrhea, and hypertension.

At age 63 years (approximately 9 months after initial systemic therapy start, 3 months after switching from combination to nivolumab monotherapy), the patient received second-line therapy with cabozantinib 60 mg orally once daily. Intent: palliative/disease control. Rationale: Progressive disease on first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab; MET amplification on tumor NGS (cabozantinib is a dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitor, potential additional benefit in MET-amplified RCC); sarcomatoid differentiation (aggressive histology warranting active TKI). Patient counseled on TKI side effect profile including HFSR, diarrhea, hypertension, fatigue, hepatotoxicity, and thyroid dysfunction. Provided with oral oncology teaching and wallet card.

At age 63 years (3 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient developed hand-foot skin reaction (HFSR, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia). CTCAE Grade 2: painful erythema, swelling, and hyperkeratosis over bilateral palms and plantar surfaces of the feet; blistering noted over the lateral border of the right foot. Interfering with activities of daily living (difficulty walking prolonged distances). Management: Cabozantinib dose reduced to 40 mg orally once daily per institutional guidelines for Grade 2 HFSR. Urea 40% cream applied twice daily to hyperkeratotic areas. Gel cushioned shoe inserts provided. Referral to dermatology for wound care of blistered area. Patient counseled to avoid tight-fitting footwear and prolonged standing. After 2 weeks at reduced dose: HFSR improved to Grade 1 (persistent hyperkeratosis but non-painful, not limiting function).

At age 63 years (5 weeks after initiating cabozantinib, concurrent with ongoing HFSR management), the patient developed diarrhea. CTCAE Grade 2: 4-6 loose stools per day over baseline, mild abdominal cramping, no blood in stool. Infectious workup negative. Not colitis on clinical assessment (no tenesmus, no urgency, no fever). Consistent with TKI-related diarrhea. Management: Loperamide 2 mg after each loose stool (up to 8 mg/day), low-residue diet, adequate hydration encouraged. Patient maintained on dose-reduced cabozantinib 40 mg. Diarrhea improved to Grade 1 (2-3 loose stools above baseline) within 10 days of initiation of loperamide and dietary modifications. Ongoing management with as-needed loperamide.

At age 63 years (8 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient was found to have new-onset hypertension at routine clinic visit. Blood pressure 158/96 mmHg (repeated measurement confirmed). Prior blood pressure well-controlled on amlodipine. CTCAE Grade 2 hypertension. Amlodipine dose increased from 5 mg to 10 mg daily. Blood pressure rechecked at 2 weeks: 138/84 mmHg (improved to Grade 1). Additional antihypertensive not required. Patient instructed on home blood pressure monitoring twice daily.

At age 63 years (3 months after initiating cabozantinib, first restaging on cabozantinib), the patient had a medical oncology assessment with restaging imaging. Cancer present. Stable disease. ECOG performance status 1. Patient tolerating cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Ongoing Grade 1 HFSR (hyperkeratosis, non-painful, managed with urea cream), Grade 1 diarrhea (1-2 loose stools above baseline, managed with as-needed loperamide), blood pressure controlled (136/82 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg). No fatigue above Grade 1. TSH 4.2 mIU/L on levothyroxine 50 mcg (euthyroid). No hepatotoxicity (LFTs within normal limits). CBC: mild anemia (Hgb 10.2 g/dL, Grade 1), no thrombocytopenia. Restaging CT: stable disease per RECIST 1.1 (sum of target lesion diameters decreased by 12% from cabozantinib baseline). Plan: Continue cabozantinib 40 mg. Repeat imaging in 3 months. Maintain current dose given tolerability. Cancer present, stable disease on cabozantinib.

At age 63 years (3-month restaging scan on cabozantinib 40 mg daily), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Cancer present. Stable disease. Pulmonary metastases: Target lesion 1 (RLL dominant): 2.3 cm (from 2.6 cm at cabozantinib baseline, -12% decrease). Target lesion 2 (RLL): 0.5 cm (stable). Target lesion 3 (LLL): 0.4 cm (stable). New pulmonary nodules (RML, previously 0.6 and 0.4 cm): 0.5 cm and 0.3 cm (both slightly decreased or stable). Right paracaval lymph node: 0.7 cm (from 1.1 cm at prior scan, decreased). No new pulmonary nodules. No new sites of metastatic disease. No adrenal, hepatic, or osseous metastases. No pleural effusion. Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.2 cm (from 3.7 cm at cabozantinib baseline, -14% change). Impression: Partial response approaching per RECIST 1.1 (not yet meeting -30% threshold). Overall stable disease with minor response in all target and non-target lesions. No progression.

At age 63 years (6-month restaging scan on cabozantinib, cumulative ~15 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Cancer present. Stable disease maintained. Target lesion 1 (RLL dominant): 2.1 cm (continued slow decrease). Target lesion 2 (RLL): 0.4 cm. Target lesion 3 (LLL): 0.4 cm. Right paracaval lymph node: 0.6 cm (near-complete response of this node). Previously noted right middle lobe nodules: 0.4 cm and 0.3 cm (both stable/minimally decreased). No new sites of disease. No pleural effusion. No osseous, hepatic, or adrenal metastases. No brain metastases on dedicated MRI brain performed concurrently. Sum of target lesion diameters: 2.9 cm (from 3.4 cm original baseline, -15% overall; -22% from cabozantinib initiation baseline of 3.7 cm). Impression: Stable disease with continued partial response to cabozantinib. No evidence of new or progressive disease.

At age 63 years (6 months on cabozantinib, approximately 15 months from original diagnosis), the patient had a medical oncology assessment. Cancer present. Stable disease. ECOG performance status 1 (maintained). Patient continues on cabozantinib 40 mg orally daily. Side effect profile remains manageable: Grade 1 HFSR (minimal hyperkeratosis, asymptomatic, well-managed with emollients), intermittent Grade 1 diarrhea controlled with as-needed loperamide, blood pressure controlled (134/80 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg). Patient reports improved energy and is able to perform most activities of daily living independently. Returns to light gardening. No significant fatigue. Endocrine: TSH 3.8 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg (dose adjusted upward at 4-month mark). Creatinine stable at 1.4 mg/dL. No proteinuria on spot urine. Restaging CT: stable disease with continued gradual tumor reduction in all target lesions. Discussion: Patient is deriving ongoing benefit from cabozantinib. No trial eligibility at present. Continue current therapy. Discussed MET amplification and potential eligibility for future trials. Next imaging in 3 months. Cancer present, stable disease.

At age 64 years (approximately 18 months from original diagnosis, 9 months on cabozantinib), the patient had an interval medical oncology assessment. Cancer present. Stable disease. Patient continues on cabozantinib 40 mg daily with maintained stable disease over successive imaging cycles. A new issue arose: patient developed low back pain (Grade 1-2) over the past 4 weeks. Examination: no focal neurological deficits. MRI lumbar spine ordered to evaluate for potential osseous metastases versus degenerative disease (patient has history of mild lumbar spondylosis per prior incidental imaging). Laboratory review: CBC shows Hgb 10.4 g/dL (stable mild anemia), ANC 2.8 x10^9/L (normal), platelets 198 x10^9/L. LFTs normal. Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL. No dose changes to cabozantinib at this time. ECOG performance status 1. Cancer present, stable. Next restaging CT scheduled in 6 weeks.

At age 64 years (prompted by new back pain, 9 months on cabozantinib), the patient had an MRI lumbar spine with and without gadolinium. Findings: Multilevel lumbar degenerative disc disease, most pronounced at L4-L5 (moderate disc height loss, facet arthropathy). No evidence of vertebral metastatic disease. No pathological compression fractures. No epidural extension or spinal cord compression. Mild foraminal narrowing at L4-L5 bilaterally from hypertrophic facet changes and disc bulge. No lytic or sclerotic vertebral lesions. Impression: Back pain most consistent with degenerative lumbar disc disease. No osseous metastatic disease.

At age 64 years (9-month restaging scan on cabozantinib, cumulative ~18 months from original diagnosis), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Cancer present. Stable disease maintained. Target lesion 1 (RLL dominant): 2.0 cm (continued gradual decrease, very slow). Target lesion 2 (RLL): 0.4 cm (stable). Target lesion 3 (LLL): 0.3 cm (minimal decrease). Right paracaval lymph node: no longer measurable (near-complete response). RML nodules: 0.3 cm and 0.2 cm (continued decrease or stable). No new pulmonary nodules. No new sites of disease. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastases. Sum of target lesion diameters: 2.7 cm (from 3.7 cm at cabozantinib baseline, -27% change; approaching but not meeting PR threshold of -30%). Impression: Stable disease with continued slow response to cabozantinib. Near-complete response of the retroperitoneal lymph node metastasis. No evidence of progressive disease.

At age 64 years (9-month cabozantinib assessment, 18 months from diagnosis), the patient had a comprehensive medical oncology assessment. Cancer present. Stable disease. Patient doing well overall on cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Back pain has improved substantially with physical therapy and an NSAID (naproxen, used cautiously given renal function). MRI spine: no osseous metastases (degenerative disease only). ECOG performance status 1. Side effects: Grade 1 HFSR only (prior Grade 2 fully resolved to Grade 1 with dose reduction and supportive care), intermittent very mild diarrhea (managed with PRN loperamide), blood pressure well-controlled. Quality of life assessment (FKSI-DRS): scores improved from prior assessment. Patient expresses satisfaction with current treatment. Creatinine 1.45 mg/dL (stable). No proteinuria. No thyroid dysfunction (TSH 3.5 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg). CBC: Hgb 10.6 g/dL (mild chronic anemia, stable). Discussion: Patient has durable stable disease approaching 10 months on cabozantinib with near-complete retroperitoneal lymph node response and continued slow but meaningful reduction in pulmonary target lesions. Continue current cabozantinib 40 mg. Reassess dose escalation back to 60 mg when Grade 1 HFSR fully resolves (patient defers dose escalation at this time). Next imaging in 3 months. Bone-modifying agent not indicated at this time (no osseous metastases). Cancer present, stable disease, patient tolerating therapy well.

documents.text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE
Genitourinary Oncology Service

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old Nigerian-American female, retired nurse, presenting to the Genitourinary Medical Oncology clinic for initial consultation and treatment planning following cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC). She is referred by Urologic Oncology for initiation of first-line systemic therapy. This represents her first formal outpatient medical oncology consultation.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Okonkwo was diagnosed approximately 6 weeks ago with stage IV (AJCC 8th edition) clear cell renal cell carcinoma of the right kidney. The diagnosis was precipitated by progressive right flank pain and hematuria of several weeks' duration, leading to cross-sectional imaging that revealed a large, heterogeneous right renal mass (14.2 x 11.8 x 10.3 cm), macroscopic tumor thrombus extending into the right renal vein (Mayo grade 2), a single right retroperitoneal hilar lymph node measuring 2.4 x 1.9 cm, and bilateral pulmonary nodules (dominant right lower lobe nodule 1.8 cm, additional bilateral nodules). CT-guided core needle biopsy of the primary mass confirmed clear cell RCC, Fuhrman grade 4 with approximately 20% sarcomatoid differentiation. PD-L1 expression by CPS was 12. Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx equivalent) revealed VHL p.R200* (pathogenic, with concurrent 3p LOH), PBRM1 p.Q1233*, SETD2 frameshift deletion, TP53 p.R175H, and high-level MET amplification (copy number 8.2). TMB was low at 3.1 mut/Mb. MSS. No germline RCC syndrome mutations identified.

The case was reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board. Given the patient's ECOG PS 1, symptomatic primary tumor, limited M1 disease burden (pulmonary only), and IMDC intermediate risk status, the consensus recommendation was for upfront cytoreductive nephrectomy followed by systemic immunotherapy combination.

She underwent right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 6 weeks ago (open approach, R0 resection, 850 mL EBL, 2 units pRBC intraoperatively). Final surgical pathology confirmed pT3b pN1 M1 clear cell RCC, Fuhrman grade 4, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation; 1 of 12 retroperitoneal lymph nodes positive (2.4 cm hilar node with extranodal extension); all surgical margins negative. Postoperative course was complicated by Grade 2 ileus (resolved by postoperative day 8) and mild Grade 1 anemia. She was discharged on postoperative day 10.

She presents today, 6 weeks following surgery, for evaluation and initiation of first-line systemic therapy. She reports gradual recovery from surgery with improving energy. She continues on oral iron supplementation for post-operative anemia. She denies ongoing flank pain, hematuria, or new symptoms. She reports mild persistent fatigue (Grade 1), which she attributes to the combination of surgery and anemia. She ambulates without assistance, performs all activities of daily living independently, and has resumed light household activities.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**

Constitutional: Mild fatigue (Grade 1). No fever, chills, or night sweats. Mild weight loss of approximately 3 lbs since surgery (likely related to surgical recovery). No significant appetite changes currently.
Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest. Mild exertional dyspnea with heavy exertion (reported as baseline). No cough, hemoptysis, or chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Bowel function has normalized since resolution of postoperative ileus. No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. No hematochezia. Appetite returning to near baseline.
Genitourinary: No hematuria post-operatively. Normal urinary stream from contralateral kidney. No dysuria.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain. No arthralgias.
Neurological: No headache, diplopia, focal weakness, or seizure activity.
Dermatological: No rash.
Endocrine: No symptoms of thyroid dysfunction. No polyuria or polydipsia beyond baseline diabetes.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.

---

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY**

1. Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma, right kidney (as above) -- newly diagnosed
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- well-controlled on metformin; last HbA1c 6.8% (per records)
3. Hypertension -- on amlodipine 5 mg daily, previously well-controlled (BP 122/76 mmHg at last primary care visit prior to diagnosis)
4. No prior history of autoimmune disease
5. No prior inflammatory bowel disease
6. No prior cardiac disease
7. No prior pulmonary disease

**SURGICAL HISTORY**

1. Right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (cytoreductive) -- approximately 6 weeks ago
2. Appendectomy -- remote (age 34)
3. Cesarean section x2 (ages 29, 32)

---

**SOCIAL HISTORY**

Ms. Okonkwo is a retired registered nurse with 30+ years of experience in hospital medicine; this background informs her detailed knowledge of her own condition and her ability to engage in nuanced shared decision-making. She is a non-smoker (lifetime non-smoker) and denies alcohol use beyond an occasional social drink (less than 1 drink/week). No illicit drug use. She is a widow; her husband passed away 8 years ago. She lives independently in a two-story home and has two adult children nearby who provide support. She is originally from Nigeria and immigrated to the United States at age 22. She follows a mixed Nigerian-American diet with moderate protein intake. She is insured (Medicare and supplemental). She expresses a strong preference to remain informed and involved in all medical decisions.

---

**FAMILY HISTORY**

Mother: Deceased, breast cancer (diagnosed in her 60s).
Father: Deceased, coronary artery disease.
Sibling (brother): Hypertension, type 2 diabetes.
No known family history of kidney cancer, hereditary RCC syndrome (VHL, Birt-Hogg-Dube, hereditary papillary RCC), Lynch syndrome, or other hereditary cancer syndromes. Germline testing of patient confirmed no pathogenic mutations in VHL, FLCN, MET, SDHB, SDHC, or SDHD.

---

**ALLERGIES**

No known drug allergies. No known food allergies.

---

**MEDICATIONS**

1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 DM)
2. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily (hypertension)

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma of the right kidney (pT3b pN1 M1, surgically debulked), characterized by Fuhrman grade 4 histology with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, PD-L1 CPS 12, MET amplification, VHL/PBRM1/SETD2/TP53 co-mutations, and low TMB. **1. First-Line Systemic Therapy: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (CheckMate 214 Regimen)**

After extensive discussion with Ms. Okonkwo and review at the multidisciplinary tumor board, I recommend initiating first-line immunotherapy combination with ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg IV plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance therapy. This recommendation is based on:

- IMDC intermediate risk (2 factors): The CheckMate 214 trial demonstrated superior overall survival and objective response rate for ipilimumab/nivolumab versus sunitinib in IMDC intermediate and poor risk ccRCC patients.
- Sarcomatoid differentiation (20%): Sarcomatoid histology represents a pre-specified subgroup with particularly pronounced benefit from dual checkpoint inhibition in the CheckMate 214 trial and in the dedicated sarcomatoid/rhabdoid ccRCC analyses. Pembrolizumab-based combinations (KEYNOTE-426 axitinib/pembro) are also active, but the sarcomatoid subgroup data and institutional experience favor ipi/nivo in this context.
- PD-L1 CPS 12: Intermediate PD-L1 expression supports immunotherapy responsiveness, though response in RCC is not strictly PD-L1 dependent.
- No contraindications to dual checkpoint blockade identified: No active autoimmune disease, no chronic systemic immunosuppression, hepatitis B/C and HIV negative, eGFR and LFTs acceptable.

Regimen specifics:
- Ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV over 30 minutes + Nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV over 30 minutes, every 3 weeks x 4 cycles
- Following completion of 4 induction cycles: Nivolumab 480 mg IV over 30 minutes every 4 weeks (flat dosing, maintenance)
- Premedications: Ondansetron 8 mg IV, acetaminophen 650 mg PO pre-infusion
- Anti-emetic prophylaxis: Ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN for 24 hours post-infusion

Detailed counseling provided regarding immune-related adverse events (irAEs) including: immune-related colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, hypophysitis, thyroiditis/hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, nephritis, myocarditis (rare but serious), dermatitis, and uveitis. Patient educated on the importance of early recognition and reporting of new symptoms. Patient was provided with immunotherapy wallet card, irAE symptom diary, and 24-hour oncology triage line number. Given her nursing background, Ms. Okonkwo demonstrated excellent understanding of immune-related toxicity recognition and management principles.

Specific counseling regarding steroid use: Patient instructed that if irAE occurs requiring high-dose corticosteroids, metformin should be held during steroid course given risk of steroid-induced hyperglycemia and lactic acidosis.

Note regarding MET amplification: Tumor NGS demonstrates high-level MET amplification (CN 8.2). While this is not actionable in the first-line setting, this finding may have therapeutic relevance in subsequent lines of therapy. Cabozantinib, a dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitor, would be a preferred second-line agent if progression occurs. This has been noted for future reference.

Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old Nigerian-American woman with a diagnosis of stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma of the right kidney, notable for high-risk histologic features including Fuhrman nuclear grade 4 and sarcomatoid differentiation comprising approximately 20% of the tumor volume. She underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 3.5 months ago (R0 resection), with postoperative course notable for Grade 2 ileus (resolved) and mild anemia managed with oral iron. Her IMDC risk classification is intermediate risk (2 factors: hemoglobin below LLN, time from diagnosis to systemic therapy less than 1 year).

Six weeks following nephrectomy, she initiated first-line systemic therapy per the CheckMate 214 regimen (ipilimumab + nivolumab) given intermediate IMDC risk and, critically, the presence of sarcomatoid differentiation - a pre-specified subgroup in CheckMate 214 in which dual checkpoint inhibition demonstrated particularly compelling benefit. PD-L1 CPS was 12 on her pre-operative biopsy specimen.

Her course on combination immunotherapy was complicated by Grade 2 immune-related colitis occurring after cycle 2, characterized by 5-7 loose watery stools per day. This was managed with oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with a prolonged 8-week taper. Colonoscopy confirmed diffuse pan-colitis without ulceration; infectious etiologies including C. difficile and CMV were excluded. Per protocol, ipilimumab was permanently discontinued given Grade 2 irAE; nivolumab was held during the active steroid course and resumed upon completion of the taper after shared decision-making with the patient. Since the steroid taper was completed approximately 2-3 weeks ago, the patient reports gradual improvement in her energy level and overall sense of well-being. She denies any recurrence of diarrhea or abdominal cramping. She notes some baseline fatigue but describes it as mild and manageable. She has resumed light activities at home, including some household tasks and brief walks in her neighborhood. Given the heterogeneous behavior of the pulmonary metastases - one lesion enlarging while others are regressing - the clinical question of pseudoprogression versus true progression of target lesion 1 is foremost in today's assessment. The overall RECIST categorization remains stable disease. The plan is to continue nivolumab and perform confirmatory repeat imaging in 6 weeks rather than prematurely discontinuing potentially effective immunotherapy.

In the context of PD-1/CTLA-4 blockade, the enlargement of target lesion 1 may represent pseudoprogression (tumor infiltration by immune cells with resultant transient enlargement) rather than true disease progression. Confirmatory imaging in 6 weeks is planned.

---

ASSESSMENT

Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (Fuhrman grade 4, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, pT3b pN1 M1) who is approximately 3 months into first-line immunotherapy. She completed 4 cycles of combination ipilimumab/nivolumab (CheckMate 214 regimen), with ipilimumab permanently discontinued after Grade 2 immune-related colitis, and has transitioned to nivolumab monotherapy maintenance (flat dose 480 mg IV q4 weeks).

1. Stage IV ccRCC - mixed/stable response on first restaging CT. The overall RECIST assessment is stable disease with a net -3% change in the sum of target lesion diameters. The key clinical challenge is the divergent behavior of the pulmonary lesions: target lesion 1 (RLL dominant, 1.8 -> 2.1 cm, +17%) has grown, while target lesions 2 and 3 have clearly regressed. This pattern is not consistent with unequivocal progression and raises genuine concern for pseudoprogression, a recognized phenomenon with PD-1/CTLA-4 therapy that may occur in approximately 5-10% of patients and is more common in patients receiving combination IO. Pseudoprogression can reflect immune cell infiltration and early tumor inflammation rather than net tumor growth. The absence of new lesions, new extrapulmonary sites, or clinical deterioration supports a watch-and-wait approach rather than premature treatment discontinuation.

2. Immune-related hypothyroidism (new) - TSH 7.8 mIU/L with low-normal free T4. This is a well-recognized irAE associated with anti-PD-1 therapy, occurring in 5-10% of patients on nivolumab, and is more common following combination ipi/nivo. The patient endorses mild symptoms consistent with hypothyroidism (cold intolerance, constipation). Levothyroxine replacement is indicated.

3. Prior Grade 2 immune-related colitis - resolved. Colonoscopy-confirmed immune-mediated pan-colitis, managed with prolonged prednisone taper. Now fully resolved (Grade 0). No evidence of recurrence. Ipilimumab permanently discontinued; nivolumab resumed.

4. Mild normocytic anemia, Grade 1 (Hgb 10.8 g/dL) - improving since post-operative period (from 9.2 g/dL). Likely multifactorial: chronic disease/inflammation, chronic kidney disease (eGFR ~46), and prior post-operative blood loss. No transfusion required.

5. Subclinical renal insufficiency (eGFR ~46 mL/min/1.73m2) - stable, expected in the setting of solitary kidney. Metformin continued with ongoing renal function monitoring; dose currently appropriate for this eGFR. No nephrotoxic agents added.

6. Diabetes mellitus type 2 - HbA1c 7.1%, well-controlled on metformin. No dose adjustment needed.

7. Hypertension - BP well-controlled today (126/80 mmHg) on amlodipine 5 mg daily. No changes needed.

---

PLAN

1. Stage IV ccRCC / Mixed response on first restaging:
   - Continue nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (flat dose maintenance). The mixed response pattern with RECIST stable disease and the temporal context of active PD-1 blockade favor continuation rather than therapy switch. True progression cannot be confirmed in the absence of new lesions and with overall stable disease sum of diameters.
   - Repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in 6 weeks (confirmatory scan specifically to evaluate target lesion 1 and to rule out emergence of new disease sites). If target lesion 1 continues to grow or new lesions emerge on this confirmatory scan, this will confirm true progression and prompt discussion of second-line therapy options (cabozantinib or lenvatinib/everolimus).
   - Continue to monitor for all irAEs at each nivolumab infusion visit (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH, glucose, and symptoms review prior to each cycle).
   - MET amplification noted on tumor NGS; this remains a potentially actionable finding for second-line therapy (cabozantinib, a dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitor, would be a particularly attractive choice in the second-line setting given this molecular context).

2. Immune-related hypothyroidism:
   - Initiate levothyroxine 50 mcg orally daily. Counsel patient to take on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before first meal or coffee.
   - Recheck TSH and free T4 in 6-8 weeks to assess response and titrate dose as needed.
   - No need to hold nivolumab for Grade 1-2 immune-related endocrinopathy managed with hormone replacement per NCCN and ASCO irAE management guidelines.
   - Counsel patient on signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism versus hyperthyroidism (rare thyroiditis flare).

3. Prior immune-related colitis (resolved):
   - No further prednisone required. Monitor for recurrence of GI symptoms. Patient instructed to contact oncology nurse immediately if diarrhea recurs, especially with nivolumab continuation.
   - Ipilimumab permanently discontinued (will not be reintroduced). Documented allergy/irAE.

4. Anemia:
   - Mild Grade 1, stable trend toward improvement. Continue monitoring on serial CBCs. Continue oral iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily with vitamin C).
   - Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent not indicated at this time. Transfusion threshold not reached.

5. Renal function:
   - Monitor creatinine and eGFR every 4-6 weeks. Maintain adequate hydration.
   - Hold metformin if eGFR falls below 30 mL/min/1.73m2; currently safe to continue at eGFR ~46.
   - Avoid nephrotoxic medications and contrast use with appropriate hydration/premedication protocols.

6. Diabetes and hypertension:
   - Continue metformin as above. Continue amlodipine 5 mg daily (blood pressure at goal today).
   - Coordinate ongoing management with primary care physician.

7. Supportive care:
   - Fatigue: Patient counseled on energy conservation strategies, graded aerobic activity (walking program as tolerated). Fatigue anticipated to improve further as immune-related events resolve and as systemic inflammation diminishes.
   - Nutritional support: Referred to oncology dietitian for protein-adequate, anti-inflammatory diet counseling.
   - Psychosocial: Brief anxiety screening today (PHQ-2 score 1, not meeting threshold for major depressive disorder). Will monitor. Social work referral offered; patient declines at this time but acknowledges support from her daughter.

8. Return to clinic:
   - Next medical oncology visit in 6 weeks, coinciding with next restaging CT (confirmatory scan for target lesion 1 assessment) and next nivolumab infusion.
   - Patient instructed to call with any new or worsening symptoms between visits: fever, diarrhea (>4 stools/day), shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, new neurological symptoms, or severe fatigue.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 45 minutes.

Pulmonary findings: The dominant right lower lobe nodule has grown to 2.6 cm from 1.8 cm at baseline and 2.1 cm at the 3-month scan — this is a definitive, sustained increase that is no longer compatible with pseudoprogression or inflammatory changes from immunotherapy. The other smaller target lesions (RLL second nodule and LLL nodule) remain essentially stable at 0.6 cm and 0.5 cm, respectively. Two new right middle lobe nodules (0.6 cm and 0.4 cm) have appeared — these were not present at baseline or the 3-month scan.

Retroperitoneal: New right paracaval lymph node measuring 1.1 cm has emerged, in the anatomical territory of the previously resected and pathologically confirmed metastatic right hilar/retroperitoneal node. This is of particular clinical relevance given the known nodal drainage patterns and the patient's pN1 disease.

No new hepatic, adrenal, or osseous lesions identified. No pleural effusion. No ascites.

Overall impression: There is unequivocal progression of disease, with growth of the dominant pulmonary metastasis, emergence of new pulmonary nodules, and new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. This is not consistent with pseudoprogression at 6 months with multiple new sites.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. Metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), right kidney primary, stage IV (pT3b pN1 M1), Fuhrman grade 4, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation — with confirmed disease progression on nivo monotherapy (following earlier discontinuation of Yervoy due to immune-related colitis after cycle 2, with only 2 cycles of combination ipi/nivo completed).

   The clinical picture is now one of true disease progression at approximately 6 months of systemic therapy. The mixed response pattern seen at 3 months (with some lesions shrinking) was ultimately insufficient, as the dominant lesion has continued its upward trajectory and new lesions have appeared at two sites — pulmonary (right middle lobe) and retroperitoneal. The patient received a truncated course of dual checkpoint inhibition (only 2 cycles ipi/nivo before ipi was held), and it is possible that the full benefit of the combination was not realized. Nevertheless, with objective new sites of disease on scan, continuing Opdivo cannot be justified.

   The molecular profile of this tumor is notable: VHL p.R200* (biallelic loss, canonical ccRCC driver), PBRM1 nonsense mutation (chromatin remodeling, associated with intermediate prognosis), SETD2 frameshift (epigenetic, associated with aggressive behavior), TP53 R175H hotspot mutation (adverse prognostic significance), and high-level MET amplification (copy number 8.2). The MET amplification is of particular therapeutic interest given that cabozantinib is a multikinase inhibitor with potent activity against both VEGFR2 and MET — a mechanistic rationale for preferential benefit in MET-amplified tumors.

2. Prior immune-related colitis (Grade 2, ipi/nivo-related) — in complete remission, no active gastrointestinal symptoms.

3. Immune-related hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 50 mcg daily — TSH now 6.1 mIU/L and free T4 low-normal, suggesting current dose may be slightly insufficient; will uptitrate.

4. Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 10.5 g/dL, Grade 1) — chronic, stable; consistent with post-nephrectomy status and chronic disease. No acute intervention required.

5. Stage 3 chronic kidney disease (eGFR ~44 mL/min/1.73m2) — stable solitary left kidney function. Renal function must be monitored carefully on any subsequent TKI therapy.

6. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well-controlled on metformin. Metformin used cautiously given eGFR; dose not exceeding 1000 mg/day per current guidelines for eGFR 45-60 mL/min.

7. Hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily, currently well-controlled (132/80 mmHg today) — noting that VEGFR-targeted TKI therapy commonly causes or exacerbates hypertension; blood pressure management plan already in place.

---

PLAN

1. DISCONTINUE nivo: Given unequivocal progressive disease at 6 months with new sites of metastasis, nivo will be discontinued today. The patient was counseled that she received meaningful, if incomplete, benefit from immunotherapy. We discussed the reasoning for stopping and the rationale for transition to a different mechanism of action.

2. INITIATE SECOND-LINE THERAPY — CABOZANTINIB 60 MG ORALLY ONCE DAILY:
   Rationale discussed with patient:
   - Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) is a standard-of-care option for second-line metastatic RCC following progression on prior immunotherapy, supported by the METEOR trial data as well as real-world evidence post-IO. In the second-line setting following IO, cabozantinib has demonstrated improved outcomes over evero.
   - Most importantly for this patient: the tumor harbors MET amplification at high copy number (8.2). Cabozantinib is one of very few approved agents with dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitory activity, and preclinical/emerging clinical data suggest potential for enhanced benefit in MET-amplified tumors, particularly in the context of sarcomatoid differentiation where MET pathway activation may contribute to the aggressive phenotype.
   - Sarcomatoid differentiation (20% of tumor) is an aggressive feature; active TKI therapy is warranted.
   - Starting dose: 60 mg orally once daily. Dose modification down to 40 mg is available for toxicity management per standard protocol.
   - Oral oncology teaching to be provided by our oncology pharmacist today; patient to receive pill card, side effect wallet card, and written instructions.

3. SIDE EFFECT COUNSELING:
   The most common toxicities of cabozantinib requiring proactive management were discussed at length:
   - Hand-foot skin reaction (HFSR/palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): Patient counseled to use thick emollient cream (urea 20-40% to any callous areas) prophylactically, avoid tight-fitting shoes and prolonged pressure on heels/balls of feet, and to contact the office immediately if blistering or painful skin breakdown occurs (Grade 2 warrants dose modification).
   - Diarrhea: Anti-motility agents (loperamide) to keep on hand; low-residue diet advice provided. If 4+ stools/day above baseline, contact clinic.
   - Hypertension: Blood pressure is currently controlled. Home blood pressure monitoring (twice daily for the first 4 weeks) strongly recommended. Amlodipine already in place; other antihypertensives can be added if needed. Grade 2 hypertension (>140/90 persistent despite optimization) may require dose hold.
   - Fatigue: Expected, typically manageable with activity pacing.
   - Hepatotoxicity: LFTs to be monitored on schedule. Patient instructed to avoid new herbal supplements, NSAIDs, or nephrotoxic agents without discussion.
   - Thyroid dysfunction: Already hypothyroid and on levothyroxine; TFTs to be followed.
   - Nausea: Typically mild; small, frequent meals advised.
   - Patient given the opportunity to ask questions; all questions addressed satisfactorily.

4. THYROID MANAGEMENT: Increase levothyroxine from 50 mcg to 75 mcg daily given persistent mild TSH elevation (6.1 mIU/L) and low-normal free T4. Repeat TFTs in 6 weeks.

5. RENAL MONITORING: Creatinine stable at 1.4 mg/dL (eGFR 44). Continue monitoring. Cabozantinib is not renally cleared at significant levels; no dose adjustment needed for this level of CKD. Hold metformin if creatinine rises above 1.8 mg/dL.

6. DIABETES MANAGEMENT: Maintain metformin 500 mg twice daily (dose-limited given eGFR). HbA1c to be obtained at next visit. Given hyperglycemia may be exacerbated by TKI or any future steroid requirements, endocrine co-management to be maintained.

7. BASELINE LABS PRIOR TO CABOZANTINIB (to be drawn today): CBC with differential, CMP, LFTs, TSH, free T4, uric acid, fasting lipids, urinalysis with spot protein-to-creatinine ratio.

8. RESTAGING PLAN: First restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis planned at approximately 3 months after cabozantinib initiation. Clinical labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH) every 3-4 weeks for first 3 months, then every 6-8 weeks thereafter per institutional protocol.

9. CLINICAL TRIALS: Reviewed currently available trials at this institution. No immediately eligible phase II or III trials for second-line MET-amplified ccRCC are actively enrolling at this time. Patient expressed willingness to consider clinical trial participation if eligible in the future. Will continue to monitor the landscape.

10. PSYCHOSOCIAL: Patient expressed some disappointment regarding disease progression but remains engaged and motivated. Reviewed the clinical rationale for the new treatment plan and expressed understanding. She lives at home with her spouse; support system appears strong. Social work referral offered; patient declines at this time but will consider. Patient advised to maintain communication with her primary care physician and endocrinologist.

1. STAGE IV CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA — STABLE DISEASE ON CABOZANTINIB (9 MONTHS ON THERAPY)
Ms. Okonkwo has now been on cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg daily for approximately 9 months and continues to demonstrate durable disease control. Today's restaging CT shows sustained disease stability — the dominant RLL lesion continues its slow decline (now 2.0 cm, down from 2.6 cm at TKI start, representing an approximately 23% reduction), the paracaval lymph node near-complete response is maintained, and all remaining target lesions are stable. There are no new sites of disease.

The overall disease picture is encouraging. While she has not met formal partial response criteria (which would require a 30% or greater reduction in the sum of target diameters), she has had consistent, meaningful tumor shrinkage on virtually all measurable lesions, with the near-disappearance of the retroperitoneal nodal metastasis being particularly gratifying. Given the presence of 20% sarcomatoid differentiation and Fuhrman grade 4 histology — features that typically confer aggressive biology — maintaining stable disease for this duration represents a clinically meaningful outcome. The MET amplification on her NGS may be contributing to cabozantinib sensitivity given its dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitory mechanism.

Plan:
- Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. At this visit, the patient again defers dose re-escalation back to 60 mg, which is a reasonable decision given that her current Grade 1 HFSR has not fully resolved, and the 40 mg dose is providing excellent disease control. We will revisit this question at subsequent visits if HFSR completely resolves.
- Repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in 3 months.
- Dedicated MRI brain in approximately 3 months (routine surveillance; last brain MRI was approximately 3 months ago, negative).
- Continue to monitor for emerging signs of progression at each visit and with interval imaging.

2. HAND-FOOT SKIN REACTION (HFSR) — GRADE 1, STABLE AND WELL-MANAGED
Grade 2 HFSR previously prompted dose reduction from 60 mg to 40 mg. On 40 mg, her HFSR has downgraded to Grade 1 (bilateral palmar and plantar hyperkeratosis, non-painful, non-limiting). Exam today confirms Grade 1 status only: dry, thickened skin without erythema, blistering, or fissuring.
Plan:
- Continue urea 40% cream twice daily to palms and plantar surfaces.
- Continue to use gel cushioned insoles and avoid prolonged standing or tight-fitting footwear.
- Reinforce skin care hygiene measures. Provide patient education handout on HFSR management.
- If Grade 1 fully resolves over the next cycle, may reconsider dose re-escalation at patient's preference.
- Dermatology follow-up as needed.

3. DIARRHEA — GRADE 1, INTERMITTENT, WELL-CONTROLLED
Intermittent 1-2 loose stools above baseline, PRN loperamide sufficient for control. No signs of colitis. Infectious etiologies excluded in prior workup; pattern consistent with TKI-related GI toxicity.
Plan:
- Continue PRN loperamide up to 8 mg/day.
- Advise low-residue diet during episodes.
- If diarrhea worsens to Grade 2 (4 or more stools above baseline, or lasting >2 days), patient instructed to call the oncology nursing line for management guidance.
- No dose reduction indicated at this time.

4. HYPERTENSION — CONTROLLED ON AMLODIPINE 10 MG
Blood pressure today 134/80 mmHg, well-controlled. Patient performs home BP monitoring twice daily as instructed. Amlodipine was uptitrated from 5 mg to 10 mg early in the cabozantinib course in response to Grade 2 hypertension.
Plan:
- Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily.
- Reinforce home BP monitoring. If BP consistently >140/90 mmHg, will add a second antihypertensive agent (ACE inhibitor or ARB would be preferred given renal protection benefit in CKD; however will use caution given solitary kidney).
- Patient instructed to report any headache, visual changes, or BP readings >160 mmHg systolic.

5. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE, STAGE 3a — STABLE
Creatinine 1.45 mg/dL, eGFR approximately 45 mL/min/1.73m2 — stable over the past several months. Expected in the setting of right radical nephrectomy (solitary kidney). No significant proteinuria on urine spot check.
Plan:
- Avoid nephrotoxic medications where possible. Continue cautious naproxen use for back pain with explicit instructions to use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration, and to hold if creatinine rises.
- Monitor creatinine and urine protein at each visit.
- Metformin continued at current dose with current renal function; will reassess if eGFR drops below 30 mL/min/1.73m2.
- No dose adjustment to cabozantinib required for current renal function.

6. LUMBAR DEGENERATIVE DISC DISEASE / BACK PAIN — IMPROVED
Back pain substantially improved with physical therapy and PRN naproxen. MRI lumbar spine confirmed degenerative disc disease (L4-L5) without metastatic disease. No focal neurological deficits on exam today.
Plan:
- Continue physical therapy.
- Use naproxen PRN at lowest effective dose with food, with close renal monitoring.
- Reinforce that new neurological symptoms (radiculopathy, leg weakness, urinary or bowel dysfunction) should prompt urgent evaluation.
- No bone-modifying agent indicated at this time (no osseous metastases identified).

7. IMMUNE-RELATED HYPOTHYROIDISM — CONTROLLED ON LEVOTHYROXINE
TSH 3.5 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily — euthyroid. No symptoms of thyroid dysfunction.
Plan:
- Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg orally daily (taken on empty stomach, at least 30 minutes before food or other medications).
- Recheck TFTs (TSH, free T4) at next visit.

8. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS — STABLE GLYCEMIC CONTROL
HbA1c 7.1% at last check; fasting glucose today 118 mg/dL. Well-controlled. Metformin 1000 mg BID continued.
Plan:
- Continue metformin 1000 mg twice daily.
- Monitor HbA1c every 3-6 months.
- Reinforce dietary modification. No further medication adjustment needed at this time.

9. ANEMIA — GRADE 1, STABLE, CHRONIC
Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, normocytic. Stable over the past several months. Multifactorial etiology: chronic disease of malignancy, prior surgical blood loss, and potentially cabozantinib-related (antiangiogenic agents can suppress erythropoiesis in some patients). On oral iron supplementation (initiated postoperatively; reviewed and continued).
Plan:
- No transfusion indicated. Continue current management.
- Monitor CBC at each visit.
- If hemoglobin falls below 9.0 g/dL or patient becomes symptomatic, will reassess for alternate etiologies and consider referral to hematology.

10. PSYCHOSOCIAL / QUALITY OF LIFE
Ms. Okonkwo expresses overall satisfaction with her current treatment and quality of life. She has returned to light gardening and regular walking. FKSI-DRS (Functional Assessment of Cancer Therapy — Kidney Symptom Index) scores have improved from prior assessment. She has a supportive husband and her adult children are involved in her care. No signs of depression or anxiety on today's assessment.
Plan:
- Continue to assess psychosocial well-being at each visit.
- Offer social work or counseling referral if patient requests.
- Encourage continuation of light physical activity within tolerance.

FOLLOW-UP: Return to clinic in approximately 3 months (or sooner if new symptoms arise). Repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain at that time. Labs (CBC, CMP, TFTs) to be obtained within 48 hours prior to next visit.

Time spent: 55 minutes; greater than half of time devoted to counseling and coordination of care.

She underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection, achieving R0 resection. Approximately 6 weeks post-nephrectomy, she initiated first-line systemic therapy with ipi 1 mg/kg plus Opdivo 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (CheckMate 214 regimen), motivated by her IMDC intermediate-risk classification, sarcomatoid histology, and PD-L1 positivity. nivo was discontinued and second-line cabozantinib 60 mg orally once daily was initiated approximately 9 months after her initial systemic therapy start. **HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 64-year-old Nigerian-American woman, retired nurse, with a history of stage IV (T3b N1 M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) of the right kidney with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, Fuhrman grade 4, now approximately 18 months from initial diagnosis and 9 months into second-line therapy with cabozantinib. She presents today for a scheduled comprehensive restaging assessment.

To briefly summarize her clinical course: At initial diagnosis, she presented with a 14.2 cm right renal mass with macroscopic tumor thrombus in the right renal vein (Mayo grade 2), a single right hilar retroperitoneal lymph node metastasis (2.4 cm), and bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 1.8 cm, right lower lobe). She underwent open cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 18 months ago, achieving an R0 resection; pathology confirmed pT3b pN1 M1, with 1 of 12 nodes positive (the right hilar node with extranodal extension). She recovered from surgery largely uneventfully, with a postoperative ileus that resolved by day 8 and mild anemia managed with oral iron.

She was initiated on first-line ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks (CheckMate 214 regimen), chosen in part because of the sarcomatoid histology, a subgroup that demonstrated particularly robust benefit in the CheckMate 214 data, and a CPS of 12 on PD-L1 testing. She completed 4 cycles of the combination. After cycle 2, she developed immune-related colitis (Grade 2) managed with oral prednisone; ipilimumab was permanently discontinued and nivolumab was restarted after steroid taper. Restaging imaging at 3 months showed a mixed response — the dominant right lower lobe (RLL) nodule appeared slightly larger (initially attributed to possible pseudoprogression), while the other target lesions decreased. Confirmatory imaging at 6 months, however, showed unambiguous progression of that dominant lesion, two new right middle lobe pulmonary nodules, and a new right paracaval lymph node, consistent with true disease progression. Nivolumab was discontinued.

Approximately 9 months into her overall course (around 6 months after diagnosis), she was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib, chosen specifically because of the MET amplification (copy number 8.2) identified on her Foundation One CDx-equivalent tumor NGS panel — cabozantinib's dual VEGFR2/MET inhibition was felt to offer particular activity given this molecular feature and her sarcomatoid histology. She was started at 60 mg orally once daily. Within the first 2 months, she developed Grade 2 hand-foot skin reaction (HFSR) with painful erythema and blistering over the lateral right foot, and the dose was reduced to 40 mg daily; HFSR improved to Grade 1. She also experienced Grade 2 diarrhea (TKI-related, not immune-mediated) managed with loperamide and dietary modification, and Grade 2 hypertension managed by uptitrating her amlodipine from 5 to 10 mg daily.

Since dose reduction to cabozantinib 40 mg, she has done very well. The 3-month restaging scan showed stable disease with a minor response in all measured lesions — the dominant RLL nodule decreased from 2.6 cm to 2.3 cm and the right paracaval node responded notably from 1.1 cm to 0.7 cm. The 6-month restaging scan confirmed continued slow but steady shrinkage across all disease sites, with the right paracaval lymph node nearly completely resolved. She has now been on cabozantinib for approximately 9 months.

At her last interval visit (4-6 weeks ago), a new complaint of low back pain (Grade 1-2) over the prior 4 weeks prompted an MRI lumbar spine, which demonstrated multilevel degenerative disc disease at L4-L5 and no evidence of vertebral or epidural metastatic disease. The back pain has responded well to physical therapy and cautious use of naproxen. Today's visit includes review of the most recent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis.

Today, the patient reports feeling overall well. She describes a modest but meaningful improvement in energy and functional capacity compared to earlier in her disease course. The back pain has substantially improved — she estimates approximately 70% better since beginning physical therapy, and she takes naproxen only occasionally now. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is well known to our GU oncology service. She was originally diagnosed with stage IV ccRCC approximately 18 months ago when she presented with symptomatic right flank pain and gross hematuria. Workup revealed a large right renal mass (14.2 cm, T3b with renal vein tumor thrombus), right retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (N1), and bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic disease. Histology was notable for Fuhrman grade 4 clear cell RCC with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation. Tumor NGS demonstrated VHL, PBRM1, and SETD2 mutations, as well as TP53 mutation and MET high-level amplification. PD-L1 CPS was 12. TMB was low. IMDC intermediate risk.

Her oncologic course has been as follows:
- Underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 6 weeks after diagnosis, achieving R0 resection. Pathology confirmed pT3b pN1 M1, Stage IV. Postoperative course was complicated by a brief grade 2 ileus, which resolved.
- Approximately 6 weeks post-nephrectomy, she initiated first-line therapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks per the CheckMate 214 regimen, given IMDC intermediate risk and sarcomatoid histology (pre-specified subgroup with improved benefit from dual checkpoint blockade). After cycle 2, she developed grade 2 immune-related colitis requiring ipilimumab discontinuation and a prolonged prednisone taper over 8 weeks. Nivolumab was continued as maintenance per shared decision-making. She additionally developed subclinical immune-related hypothyroidism and was started on levothyroxine.
- Restaging imaging at 3 months showed a mixed response: the dominant right lower lobe (RLL) pulmonary nodule increased slightly (raising the question of pseudoprogression), while smaller nodules decreased. She was continued on nivolumab with close surveillance.
- Confirmatory imaging at 6 months demonstrated unambiguous progression of the dominant RLL nodule, two new right middle lobe (RML) nodules, and a new right paracaval lymph node, confirming true progression. Nivolumab was discontinued.
- She was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib 60 mg orally once daily approximately 9 months after her initial systemic therapy start. Cabozantinib was selected given progression on prior immunotherapy, the presence of MET amplification on NGS (cabozantinib is a dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitor), and aggressive sarcomatoid histology. Early in her cabozantinib course, she developed grade 2 HFSR with blistering, and the dose was reduced to 40 mg daily. She subsequently developed grade 2 TKI-related diarrhea and new-onset grade 2 hypertension, both managed with loperamide/dietary modification and amlodipine dose escalation (5 mg to 10 mg), respectively.
- At her 3-month cabozantinib assessment, restaging CT showed stable disease with a modest reduction in all target lesions (sum of diameters -14% from cabozantinib baseline).

MRI lumbar spine confirmed multilevel degenerative disc disease without osseous metastases. The restaging CT performed concurrently confirmed ongoing stable disease with continued gradual tumor reduction. The dominant RLL nodule measured 2.0 cm (down from 2.6 cm at cabozantinib initiation baseline). Sum of target lesion diameters was 2.7 cm, a 27% decrease from cabozantinib baseline, approaching but not yet meeting formal partial response threshold.

The dominant right lower lobe pulmonary nodule has decreased from 2.6 cm to 2.0 cm. The previously progressive retroperitoneal lymph node has essentially disappeared. No new sites of disease have emerged since cabozantinib initiation. While formal RECIST partial response criteria have not yet been met (the sum of target lesions has fallen 27%, just below the 30% threshold), this is clinically meaningful disease control in a patient with aggressive histology (sarcomatoid, grade 4, MET-amplified). She presents with a large (14.2 cm) heterogeneous right renal mass with macroscopic tumor thrombus extending into the right renal vein (grade 2 Mayo venous thrombus), a right retroperitoneal lymph node measuring 2.4 cm at the renal hilum, and bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 1.8 cm, right lower lobe). GROSS PATHOLOGY

Received in formalin are three (3) tan-white to tan-yellow cylindrical core needle biopsy fragments ranging from 1.2 to 1.6 cm in length and 1 to 2 mm in maximum diameter. The cores are variably firm; two cores have areas of yellow-orange discoloration with focal glistening cut surfaces, consistent with lipid-rich neoplastic tissue. One core exhibits a gray-white, firm, fibrous region occupying approximately 20-25% of its length. No grossly identifiable hemorrhage or fresh necrosis in the submitted biopsy fragments, though one core has a focal dark red area at its tip. All three cores are entirely submitted in a single cassette labeled "right renal mass biopsy."

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION

All three cores are evaluable and show adequate tumor tissue for diagnosis. The overall architecture is well-sampled.

Clear Cell Component (approximately 80% of sampled tissue):
The predominant component consists of nests and alveolar clusters of polygonal cells with abundant optically clear to lightly eosinophilic cytoplasm. The cytoplasmic clarity is due to dissolution of intracytoplasmic glycogen and lipid during standard processing. Cells are arranged in compact acinar nests and sheets separated by a delicate, arborizing network of thin-walled, sinusoidal blood vessels — the classic "chicken-wire" vascular pattern characteristic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma. Nuclei are markedly enlarged and pleomorphic with prominent macronucleoli readily visible at low-power magnification (Fuhrman/ISUP nuclear grade 4 features). Prominent nucleoli are present in virtually all tumor cells, measuring greater than the diameter of a red blood cell on 10x objective. Intranuclear pseudoinclusions are identified focally. Scattered mitotic figures are present throughout, including atypical tripolar forms. Focal geographic areas of coagulative tumor necrosis are present within the clear cell component. Hemorrhage within the vascular spaces and intratumoral hemorrhage are also noted.

Sarcomatoid Component (approximately 20% of sampled tissue):
In approximately 20% of the submitted biopsy material — predominantly within one core — there is an abrupt transition from the conventional clear cell architecture to a high-grade spindle cell neoplasm with sarcomatoid morphology. This zone is composed of interlacing fascicles and storiform arrays of markedly atypical spindled cells with scant cytoplasm, irregular hyperchromatic nuclei, and conspicuous nucleoli. The mitotic rate in the sarcomatoid component is markedly elevated, with up to 8 mitotic figures per 10 high-power fields, including atypical forms. Focal geographic necrosis is prominent in the sarcomatoid areas, with necrotic foci abutting viable sarcomatoid tumor. No convincing rhabdoid differentiation is identified (no cells with eccentric eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusions or vesicular nuclei with prominent nucleoli in a rhabdoid pattern are seen on careful review of all three cores).

Additional Microscopic Findings:
Lymphocytic infiltrates, predominantly CD3+ T cells by morphology, are present at the interface between the clear cell and sarcomatoid zones. No granulomatous inflammation. No spindle cell lesions morphologically resembling dedifferentiated liposarcoma or synovial sarcoma are seen. No heterologous elements (osteosarcomatous, chondrosarcomatous, or rhabdomyosarcomatous differentiation) are identified.

FINAL PATHOLOGIC DIAGNOSIS

RIGHT RENAL MASS, CT-GUIDED CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY (3 CORES):

   CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA
   — Fuhrman Nuclear Grade: 4 (ISUP/WHO Grade 4)
   — With sarcomatoid differentiation comprising approximately 20% of sampled tumor volume

DIAGNOSTIC COMMENT / PATHOLOGIST'S NOTE

The morphologic and immunophenotypic features are diagnostic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) with high-grade sarcomatoid dedifferentiation. The immunoprofile — strong, diffuse CAIX positivity; PAX8 positivity; CD10 positivity; TFE3 and TFEB negativity — is characteristic of ccRCC arising from the proximal renal tubular epithelium with biallelic VHL inactivation (the expected molecular event, consistent with the radiologic and clinical presentation).

The sarcomatoid component (approximately 20% of sampled tissue) represents a form of epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and high-grade dedifferentiation that is well-recognized in RCC (occurring in approximately 5% of all RCC cases). Sarcomatoid differentiation in RCC is not a separate histologic entity but rather a dedifferentiation pattern that may arise in any RCC subtype, most commonly ccRCC. Its presence is associated with a significantly worse prognosis and with enhanced responsiveness to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy in prospective clinical trials (notably, in the CheckMate 214 ipilimumab/nivolumab trial, the sarcomatoid subgroup derived particular benefit from combination immunotherapy versus sunitinib monotherapy).

The PD-L1 CPS of 12 (moderate expression) is consistent with the immunologically active tumor microenvironment typical of sarcomatoid RCC.

This biopsy is diagnostic and is consistent with the imaging findings of a large, heterogeneous right renal mass with necrosis and vascular thrombus. Correlation with clinical, radiologic, and molecular data is recommended in the multidisciplinary context.

Correlation with comprehensive molecular/genomic profiling (NGS) is noted to be pending/ordered as per urologic oncology request.

1. VHL p.R200* (c.598C>T) -- PATHOGENIC NONSENSE MUTATION

   Variant class: Nonsense (stop-gain)
   Variant allele frequency (VAF): 48%
   Zygosity: Hemizygous / effectively homozygous loss (biallelic inactivation confirmed by concurrent loss of heterozygosity [LOH] at chromosome 3p25.3; see copy number section)
   Clinical significance: Pathogenic. VHL is the canonical driver mutation in clear cell RCC, occurring in approximately 75-80% of sporadic ccRCC cases. Biallelic inactivation of VHL leads to constitutive stabilization of HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha transcription factors, driving upregulation of angiogenic (VEGF, PDGF) and metabolic target genes. This alteration confirms the diagnosis of clear cell RCC at the molecular level and explains the tumor's inherent sensitivity to VEGF pathway inhibitors. The p.R200* nonsense mutation has been previously reported in COSMIC and ClinVar as a pathogenic driver in ccRCC.
   Germline correlation: Germline VHL testing was performed (see germline section); no pathogenic germline VHL alteration identified. This mutation is interpreted as somatic.

   Gene: PBRM1 (Polybromo-1; SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex subunit)
   Variant class: Nonsense (stop-gain)
   Variant allele frequency (VAF): 42%
   Zygosity: Hemizygous / biallelic loss (concurrent LOH at chromosome 3p21.1; see copy number section)
   Clinical significance: Pathogenic. PBRM1 is the second most commonly mutated gene in ccRCC, altered in approximately 40-50% of cases. It encodes the BAF180 subunit of the PBAF chromatin remodeling complex, functioning as a tumor suppressor. Loss of PBRM1 has been associated with altered immune microenvironment signaling and, in some retrospective analyses of Opdivo-treated ccRCC patients, has been correlated with differential response to immune checkpoint inhibition (specifically, loss-of-function PBRM1 mutations have been associated with improved outcomes with anti-PD-1 therapy in some datasets). The co-occurrence of PBRM1 and VHL loss on the same chromosome 3p deletion event is a defining molecular feature of clear cell RCC.

   Clinical significance: Pathogenic. SETD2 encodes the sole trimethylase responsible for H3K36me3 histone modification, a mark associated with active transcription and DNA mismatch repair fidelity.    Clinical significance: Pathogenic. The p.R175H substitution is one of the most frequently observed and well-characterized TP53 hotspot mutations across human cancers.
